_meta.purpose: Machinery a downstream user could apply to convert whole-organ or whole-body flux to per-gram-liver or per-hepatocyte units. Provided as a documented scaffold, NOT executed.
_meta.critical_warning: None of these constants were retrieved as primary sources during this session. They are widely-used standard reference-physiology values named here so the user can independently verify each against the cited source before use. They are NOT interchangeable with the verbatim MEASURED values in measured_records.json.
_meta.conversion_policy: NO per-hepatocyte conversion is performed anywhere in this dataset. Per the anti-fabrication rule, a per-cell value is computed only if ONE paper supplies EVERY denominator (organ mass, hepatocellularity, cell volume, flow) for its OWN cohort. No session-retrieved paper does. Therefore all per-hepatocyte fields resolve to null by necessity.

constants.name: adult_liver_mass
constants.standard_reference: ICRP Publication 89 (Reference Man/Woman): ~1800 g male, ~1400 g female
constants.role_in_conversion: scales whole-organ flux to per-gram; converts per-kg-body-weight rates to hepatic totals via body mass

constants.name: hepatocellularity_HPGL
constants.standard_reference: Barter et al. 2007, Curr Drug Metab (pooled human HPGL ~99 x10^6/g); also Sohlenius-Sternbeck 2006
constants.role_in_conversion: converts per-gram-liver flux to per-hepatocyte; MOST uncertain denominator

constants.name: total_hepatocyte_number
constants.plausible_range: ~1.4-2.4 x10^11 (= liver_mass x HPGL x hepatocyte_fraction)
constants.standard_reference: Derived product (liver_mass x HPGL); commonly cited ~2.4 x10^11

constants.name: hepatocyte_volume_fraction_of_liver
constants.plausible_range: 0.78-0.80 (hepatocytes ~78% of liver volume, ~60% of cell number)
constants.standard_reference: Blouin et al. 1977 J Cell Biol (rat stereology, widely extrapolated); human values sparse

constants.name: single_hepatocyte_volume
constants.standard_reference: Multiple; wide range reported. No consensus single human value.
constants.role_in_conversion: converts per-cell amount to intracellular concentration; needed for flux-per-cell-volume

constants.name: total_hepatic_blood_flow
constants.plausible_range: 1.0-1.8 L/min (~25% of cardiac output at rest)
constants.standard_reference: Standard physiology; often measured by ICG/dye clearance in the source papers themselves
constants.verification_status: NOT_retrieved_this_session_as_constant; several source papers report their own measured flow
constants.role_in_conversion: converts A-V concentration difference (mM) to net flux (concentration x flow)

constants.name: portal_vein_flow_fraction
constants.role_in_conversion: partitions splanchnic inflow between portal and arterial supply

constants.name: assumed_body_weight
constants.plausible_range: study-specific; use each cohort's actual mean when reported
constants.role_in_conversion: converts µmol/kg/min (as reported by most tracer studies) to µmol/min whole-body

conversion_formulae.formula: net_hepatic_flux (µmol/min) = (A - HV concentration, mM) x hepatic_blood_flow (L/min) x 1000
conversion_formulae.applicable: Only for papers reporting BOTH A-HV difference AND their own measured flow. Most session sources report EITHER a balance OR a concentration, not both in accessible text.
liver_mass never measured per-subject in session sources -> would rely on unverified constant.
NEVER executed. No session paper supplies HPGL for its own cohort; chaining unverified constants would fabricate precision.
Usable when a paper reports the cohort's mean body weight; several do not in the accessible abstract.

why_per_cell_is_null_everywhere: Human in-vivo methods measure ORGAN or WHOLE-BODY flux (catheter A-V balance, or systemic tracer Ra), never single-cell flux.
Chaining F1->F2->F3 requires liver_mass + HPGL + hepatocyte_fraction, none measured per-cohort in session sources.
Hepatocellularity and single-cell volume are themselves high-variance, largely rodent-derived or drug-metabolism-pooled constants.
Portal-vein concentrations (needed for true hepatic input) are obtainable only in surgical cohorts (18703642, 2189767) that are NOT healthy fasting volunteers.
Therefore per-hepatocyte flux is unidentifiable both in principle (no method) and in practice (no complete denominator set).
